rationale: This page contains detailed information on the legal framework for the disqualification of legislators, a new constitutional amendment bill regarding the removal of ministers upon detention, and the structure and types of Parliamentary Committees. It is rich in factual and legal content suitable for question generation.
cleanedText:
Existing Legal and Judicial Framework for Disqualification after crimes
- RPA, 1951: Section 8(3) of RPA, legislators is disqualified from contesting elections or continuing in office upon conviction for certain criminal offenses with imprisonment of two years or more.
- It includes conviction for two years or more in crimes like bribery, rape, promoting enmity, or untouchability, corruption, disloyalty, etc.
- Lily Thomas v Union of India, 2013: Supreme Court ruled that lawmaker is immediately disqualified from attracting such conviction.
- Manoj Narula v. Union of India, 2014: Court held that appointing Ministers with criminal backgrounds are not legally barred, but advised PM to avoid selecting individuals with serious criminal antecedents.
- PIL of Public Interest Foundation, 2018: Courts cannot add new disqualification grounds. Recommended Parliament that political parties revoke membership and deny tickets to candidate with serious criminal cases.
1.6.1. REMOVAL OF MINISTERS UPON DETENTION
Why in the News?
Union Home Minister introduced the Constitution (One Hundred and Thirtieth Amendment) Bill, 2025 for removal of ministers upon detention in the Lok Sabha.
Key provisions of Constitution Amendment Bill, 2025
- Grounds for Removal: Union Minister, Chief Minister, State Minister, or Prime Minister to be removed if arrested and detained for 30 consecutive days for an offence punishable with 5 years or more imprisonment.
- The Bills propose significant amendments to Articles 75 (provisions of Union Ministers), 164 (provisions of State Ministers), and 239AA (Provisions for NCT Delhi) of the Indian Constitution.
Procedure for Removal:
- Union Ministers (except PM): President removes Minister on PM’s advice by the 31st day; otherwise, automatic cessation.
- State Ministers (except CM): Governor removes on CM’s advice.
- Delhi Ministers (except CM): President acts on Delhi CM’s advice.
- Prime Minister / Chief Ministers: Must resign by 31st day of custody; failure leads to automatic removal.
- Reappointment: No bar on reappointment after release from custody.
1.7. PARLIAMENTARY COMMITTEES
Why in the news?
Recently, Lok Sabha Speaker stated that Parliamentary Committees are not adversarial to the government but act as supportive and corrective instruments that offer constructive guidance.
About Parliamentary Committees (PCs)
- PC is appointed or elected by House or nominated by Speaker and which works under direction of Speaker.
- It presents its report to House or Speaker.
- PCs have their origins in British Parliament.
- They draw their authority from Article 105 (deals with privileges of MPs), and Article 118 (gives Parliament authority to make rules to regulate its procedure and conduct of business).
Types of Parliamentary Committees
- Ad hoc Committee: Appointed for specific purpose and cease to exist when they finish task assigned to them. E.g. Railway Convention Committee etc.
- Standing Committee: Permanent and regular committees which are constituted from time to time. E.g. Financial Committees, Committee on Subordinate legislation etc.
